title: FTC AI Guidance and Enforcement Framework
official_title: Federal Trade Commission guidance and enforcement actions on artificial intelligence, algorithmic systems, and automated decision-making
status: Active — guidance documents plus active enforcement
overview: The FTC regulates AI under its existing authority to prohibit unfair or deceptive acts or practices (Section 5 FTC Act), the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA), the Equal Credit Opportunity Act (ECOA), and other statutes. The FTC has issued multiple guidance documents and has brought enforcement actions against companies for deceptive AI claims and harmful algorithmic systems. Companies should treat FTC guidance as quasi-regulatory — the FTC has demonstrated willingness to pursue significant actions against AI-related harms.

key_guidance_documents.title: Luring Consumers with AI Claims
key_points: Claims about AI capabilities must be truthful and substantiated
Overstating AI accuracy or effectiveness is deceptive under Section 5
AI products cannot be marketed as providing results they cannot deliver

key_guidance_documents.title: AI Claim Substantiation
key_points: Objective AI performance claims require competent and reliable scientific evidence
Endorsements of AI products must reflect genuine, verified experience
Disclosures of AI limitations must be clear and conspicuous

key_guidance_documents.title: Dealing with AI Hype: Practical Tips for Business
key_points: Do not claim AI can do something it cannot
Ensure third-party AI tools used in products meet FTC truthfulness standards
Test AI systems for discriminatory outputs before deployment

key_guidance_documents.title: Using AI Tools to Assess Employee Work
key_points: Employees surveilled or evaluated by AI must be informed
AI-based performance assessments that trigger adverse employment decisions must comply with existing anti-discrimination law
Automated systems that disproportionately disadvantage protected classes raise ECOA/Title VII concerns

enforcement_principles.description: Any statement about AI capabilities — accuracy, speed, neutrality, objectivity — must be truthful and substantiated. The FTC specifically targets 'AI washing': marketing ordinary software as AI or overstating AI capabilities to gain consumer trust.

AI systems that produce discriminatory outcomes in credit, employment, housing, education, or healthcare violate existing anti-discrimination laws enforced by the FTC. Disparate impact does not require discriminatory intent.

Using data collected through deceptive means to train AI systems, or using AI to facilitate unlawful data collection, violates Section 5. The FTC's Rite Aid and Rite Aid facial recognition action set precedent.
Using AI to analyse sensitive personal information — health, financial, biometric — without appropriate disclosure and consent raises both Section 5 and FCRA concerns.

fcra_obligations_for_ai: If an AI system constitutes a consumer reporting agency (CRA) or produces consumer reports, FCRA obligations apply
AI used for employment, credit, insurance, or housing decisions may trigger FCRA adverse action notice requirements
Employers using AI background check or screening tools must provide FCRA disclosures and obtain consent
AI scoring or risk assessment products sold to businesses for consumer-related decisions are likely consumer reports subject to FCRA accuracy and dispute obligations

notable_enforcement_actions.issue: Deployed facial recognition AI that falsely flagged customers as shoplifters; disproportionate impact on minorities. FTC banned use of facial recognition AI for 5 years.
Alexa voice data retained and used to train AI despite parents' deletion requests; $25M civil penalty.
ID verification AI produced inaccurate consumer reports without FCRA safeguards.

cross_references.relevance: AI systems producing consumer reports or used in credit/employment/housing decisions are subject to FCRA; adverse action notices required when AI-based decisions harm consumers
AI systems used in credit decisions must not discriminate on the basis of race, sex, religion, national origin, age, marital status, or receipt of public assistance
Companies operating in both US and EU face both FTC scrutiny (deceptive claims, discrimination) and EU AI Act obligations (high-risk classification, conformity assessment)
